title: Nathan M. Muncy, Ph.D.
text:
The Muncy Neuroimaging Research Lab (MNRL) is part of the Department of Psychology at the University of Notre Dame and we are affiliated with the Decision Neuroscience Lab and the Human Neuroimaging Center.
We conduct primary research using advanced computing, imaging, and statistical techniques on topics such as decision making and traumatic brain injury. We support the Notre Dame Human Neuroimaging Initiative by building multidisciplinary collaborations that incorporate MRI techniques in their research programs.

title: Nathan M. Muncy, Ph.D.
text:
The Muncy Neuroimaging Research Lab (MNRL) is part of the Department of Psychology at the University of Notre Dame and we are affiliated with the Decision Neuroscience Lab and the Human Neuroimaging Center.
We conduct primary research using advanced computing, imaging, and statistical techniques on topics such as decision making and traumatic brain injury. We support the Notre Dame Human Neuroimaging Initiative by building multidisciplinary collaborations that incorporate MRI techniques in their research programs.

title: About
text: Our research is driven by the philosophy that context (e.g. structural, socioeconomic, geographic, etc.) interacts with neurobiology to influence both how a person recovers from injury; these factors don’t determine a person’s path, but they do significantly shift the probabilities of how they flourish.

title: About
text:
Research Philosophy & Interests
We are interested in translational research that bridges the gap between the environment and biology. By modeling the interplay between socioeconomic factors, neurological health, and cognitive performance, we aim to understand why some individuals flourish while others struggle following a Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI). Our goal is to identify the critical environmental and biological levers that drive recovery and long-term well-being.

title: About
text:
Key Contributions & Methodologies
Our lab leverages advanced computational techniques and massive datasets to redefine how we understand brain architecture and injury:

Methodological Innovation: We utilize Topologic Data Analysis (TDA) to move beyond traditional discrete thresholding. This provides a multiscale, topologic description of neural systems, allowing us to identify phase transitions and persistent features in brain organization.
Advanced Statistics: To handle the heterogeneity of real-world recovery data, we employ Generalized Additive Models (GAMs). This allows us to resolve complex non-linear interactions and non-Gaussian distributions inherent in longitudinal TBI research.
Large-Scale Data Science: Our findings are grounded in high-powered analysis, leveraging national databases with a combined total of over 32,750 subjects.
Holistic Modeling: We don’t just look at scans; we look at the world. Our research incorporates geospatial and demographic contexts to understand how variables like geography and socioeconomic status create differential recovery outcomes.

title: About
text:
Institutional Support & Mentorship
Beyond independent research, Dr. Muncy is committed to elevating the university’s research infrastructure. We support the Neuroscience and Behavior component of Notre Dame’s Health and Well-Being Initiative through:

Technical Oversight: Implementing and refining MRI research programs across the department.
Mentorship: Providing instruction and shared reference materials for researchers navigating core MRI methodologies.

title: Research
text: Our work centers on four primary pillars: advancing network neuroscience through novel frameworks, identifying sources of variability in traumatic brain injury (TBI) recovery, supporting interdisciplinary collaborations via advanced analytics, and providing mentorship and technical expertise for the university’s imaging infrastructure.

title: Research
text:
1. Network Neuroscience: Topological Data Analysis (TDA)
We are developing a framework to characterize how the brain’s structural and functional organization relates to complex behavior. Using large, open-source datasets, we apply Topological Data Analysis (TDA) to model MRI network properties.
Unlike traditional discrete thresholding, TDA allows us to:

Capture the evolution of topological features across a continuum of edge densities.
Quantify shape-based properties and identify Euler singularities—points where global organizational properties shift abruptly.
Link these phase transitions to age, cognitive performance, and clinical symptoms.

title: Research
text:
2. TBI: Modeling Recovery Outcomes
We utilize Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) to navigate the high dimensionality and non-linear interactions inherent in neuroimaging and clinical data.

Pathophysiology & Biomarkers: Using a dataset of ~2,150 patients, we employ GAMs and tensor product interaction smooths to model longitudinal white matter injury and recovery. This helps identify specific diffusion-based biomarkers that correlate with clinical assessments.
Socio-Demographic & Geospatial Factors: In a larger cohort of ~30,000 patients, we investigate the variance in recovery timelines. Recovery can range from days (mild TBI) to years (severe TBI). We model how factors such as age at injury, education and other structural factors, sex, and geographic location (latitude/longitude) interact with socioeconomic status to influence long-term outcomes.

title: Research
text:
3. Interdisciplinary Collaboration & Advanced Analytics
We serve as a computational and methodological hub, bridging the gap between high-dimensional neuroimaging data and diverse clinical and psychological applications. By coordinating among diverse groups, we enable researchers to leverage complex datasets.

Multi-modal Data Fusion: We implement advanced integration techniques to combine fMRI, DTI, and behavioral metrics, uncovering latent patterns that a single modality would miss.
Reproducible Workflows & Open Science: To ensure cross-departmental reliability, we standardize pipelines, and containerized environments (Docker/Singularity). This reduces needless researcher degrees of freedom and ensures that collaborative results are robust and replicable.
Scalable Analytics via CRC: We facilitate the transition from desktop-level analysis to high-performance computing (HPC). By optimizing code for Notre Dame’s Center for Research Computing (CRC), we allow collaborators to process massive datasets at scale.

title: Research
text:
4. Mentorship & Neuroimaging Infrastructure Support
We are committed to sustaining the university’s imaging ecosystem by providing the technical expertise necessary for high-fidelity data acquisition and training the next generation of neuroimagers.

Pedagogical Technical Training: Beyond theoretical mentorship, we provide hands-on training in neuroimaging physics, preprocessing logic, and signal processing. Our goal is to transform trainees from black-box tool users into researchers who understand the underlying processes which transform their data.
Consultative Methodological Support: We act as a resource for the broader university community, assisting in the design of longitudinal imaging studies and power analyses to ensure that nascent projects are methodologically sound and competitive for federal funding (NIH/NSF).
